source_paper_title: Online Bipartite Matching with Reusable Resources
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Delong_2024, title={Online Bipartite Matching with Reusable Resources}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0242}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0242}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Delong, Steven and Farhadi, Alireza and Niazadeh, Rad and Sivan, Balasubramanian and Udwani, Rajan}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1825–1854} }
context:
Page 27, Section 5 (Conclusion), paragraph titled “problems”

The main problem left unanswered in our paper is whether one can obtain the competitive ratio of \(1 - 1/e\) for OBMRR.
title: Achieve the optimal 1 minus 1 over e competitive ratio in reusable-resource matching
background_latex:
Online bipartite matching with reusable resources (OBMRR) is defined on a bipartite graph \(G=(V,U;E)\), where \(V\) is a set of offline vertices (reusable resources) and \(U\) is a sequence of online vertices (requests) arriving over discrete times \(j=1,2,\dots,|U|\) in an oblivious adversarial order. When request \(j\) arrives, its incident edges \(\{(i,j)\in E: i\in V\}\) are revealed, and an online algorithm must either match \(j\) to an available neighbor \(i\in V\) or leave \(j\) unmatched; decisions are irrevocable.

Reusability is governed by a known deterministic usage duration \(d\in\mathbb{N}\): if resource \(i\) is matched at time \(j\), then \(i\) becomes unavailable for the next \(d-1\) arrivals and becomes available again at time \(j+d\). Equivalently, each resource \(i\) can be matched at most once in any window of \(d\) consecutive times.

In the vertex-weighted version, each resource \(i\in V\) has a nonnegative weight \(r_i\). Each time \(i\) is matched, the algorithm earns reward \(r_i\). The total reward of an algorithm is the sum of rewards over all matches it makes.

Let \(\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)\) denote the (expected) total reward obtained by a (possibly randomized) online algorithm on instance \((G,d)\), where the expectation is over the algorithm's internal randomness. Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\) denote the maximum total reward achievable by an offline algorithm with full knowledge of the entire arrival sequence and graph, subject to the same reusability constraints.

The competitive ratio of an online algorithm is the largest \(\Gamma\in[0,1]\) such that for all instances \((G,d)\), \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)]\ge \Gamma\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\). For the classic non-reusable online bipartite matching (obtained as a special case when \(d=|U|\)), the optimal competitive ratio is \(1-1/e\) for randomized algorithms, and this value also serves as an upper bound for OBMRR because OBMRR generalizes the non-reusable model (for large \(d\), it reduces to the classic case).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a randomized online algorithm for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) that achieves competitive ratio \(\Gamma = 1-1/e\). That is, does there exist an online algorithm such that for every instance \((G=(V,U;E),d,\{r_i\}_{i\in V})\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)] \;\ge\; (1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)?
\]
If not, determine the optimal achievable competitive ratio for OBMRR.
latex:
Online bipartite matching with reusable resources (OBMRR) is defined on a bipartite graph \(G=(V,U;E)\), where \(V\) is a set of offline vertices (reusable resources) and \(U\) is a sequence of online vertices (requests) arriving over discrete times \(j=1,2,\dots,|U|\) in an oblivious adversarial order. When request \(j\) arrives, its incident edges \(\{(i,j)\in E: i\in V\}\) are revealed, and an online algorithm must either match \(j\) to an available neighbor \(i\in V\) or leave \(j\) unmatched; decisions are irrevocable.

Reusability is governed by a known deterministic usage duration \(d\in\mathbb{N}\): if resource \(i\) is matched at time \(j\), then \(i\) becomes unavailable for the next \(d-1\) arrivals and becomes available again at time \(j+d\). Equivalently, each resource \(i\) can be matched at most once in any window of \(d\) consecutive times.

In the vertex-weighted version, each resource \(i\in V\) has a nonnegative weight \(r_i\). Each time \(i\) is matched, the algorithm earns reward \(r_i\). The total reward of an algorithm is the sum of rewards over all matches it makes.

Let \(\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)\) denote the (expected) total reward obtained by a (possibly randomized) online algorithm on instance \((G,d)\), where the expectation is over the algorithm's internal randomness. Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\) denote the maximum total reward achievable by an offline algorithm with full knowledge of the entire arrival sequence and graph, subject to the same reusability constraints.

The competitive ratio of an online algorithm is the largest \(\Gamma\in[0,1]\) such that for all instances \((G,d)\), \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)]\ge \Gamma\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\). Determine whether there exists a randomized online algorithm for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) that achieves competitive ratio \(\Gamma = 1-1/e\). That is, does there exist an online algorithm such that for every instance \((G=(V,U;E),d,\{r_i\}_{i\in V})\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)] \;\ge\; (1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)?
\]
If not, determine the optimal achievable competitive ratio for OBMRR.
open_problem_quote: The main problem left unanswered in our paper is whether one can obtain the competitive ratio of \(1 - 1/e\) for OBMRR.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for hard instances (graphs and arrival orders) and estimate the empirical competitive ratio of candidate algorithms via simulation, but certifying an upper bound against all algorithms or proving tightness is not directly amenable to finite search. Still, automated discovery may uncover candidate lower-bound instances that refute specific algorithmic approaches toward \(1-1/e\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate online policies can be parameterized (e.g., priority scores, reranking schedules, correlated selection rules) and evaluated on generated adversarial or worst-case-seeking instance distributions using a computable fitness signal such as estimated worst-case competitive ratio over a test suite. An evolutionary loop could iteratively improve algorithm designs or produce informative adversarial families that challenge \(1-1/e\) aspirations.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The OBMRR model of Delong et al. generalizes classic online bipartite matching by allowing each offline vertex to be reused after a deterministic duration \(d\), equivalently enforcing that each resource is matched at most once in any length-\(d\) window. Because OBMRR reduces to non-reusable OBM when \(d=|U|\), the optimal \(1-1/e\) bound from RANKING and its vertex-weighted extension (Perturbed Greedy) remains an upper bound in that regime. The source paper gives the first integral (unit-inventory) algorithms beating the \(1/2\) greedy barrier: Periodic Reranking achieves about \(0.589\) via a reranking-every-\(d\)-steps variant of Perturbed Greedy analyzed through randomized dual fitting, and an OCR-based primal-dual method achieves \(50/99\approx 0.505\) by extending online correlated selection to reusable resources.

At present, there is no evidence in the provided material that the open problem of achieving \(1-1/e\) for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic \(d\) has been solved. The main technical obstacles highlighted by Delong et al. are (i) long-range dependencies created by reuse (a fixed ranking can force repeated use of the same resource across far-apart times), and (ii) the need for stronger, availability-aware negative correlation across rounds within distance \(d-1\). Their Periodic Reranking partially breaks these dependencies by resampling ranks on a period aligned with \(d\), but the analysis loses slack precisely in scenarios where a resource is unavailable at time \(j\) due to being used late in the previous period. Their OCR framework provides a modular way to inject negative correlation, but with a relatively small \(\gamma\) parameter (\(1/32\)) leading only to a slight improvement over \(1/2\).

Promising directions suggested by the surrounding literature are to strengthen the correlated-selection primitive and/or allow richer query structures. Subsequent work on improved OCS and multiway OCS indicates that better correlation guarantees are possible in non-reusable settings; translating these improvements to the reusable OCR setting could increase \(\gamma\) and thus the competitive ratio of OCR-based primal-dual algorithms. Another direction is to develop new dual-fitting certificates that more tightly track availability windows (potentially beyond the two-period conditioning used in the Periodic Reranking analysis), aiming to close the gap between \(0.589\) and \(1-1/e\) or to prove a strictly smaller upper bound for integral OBMRR when \(d\) is finite.

sources.title: An optimal algorithm for on-line bipartite matching
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the RANKING algorithm for online bipartite matching with non-reusable offline vertices and proves the optimal randomized competitive ratio \(1-1/e\) against an oblivious adversary.
sources.connection: OBMRR reduces to classic OBM when \(d=|U|\); thus \(1-1/e\) is an upper bound for OBMRR in that regime and the techniques (random ranking/negative correlation) motivate attempts to reach \(1-1/e\) with reusability.

sources.title: Online vertex-weighted bipartite matching and single-bid budgeted allocations
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives the Perturbed Greedy algorithm for vertex-weighted online bipartite matching with non-reusable offline vertices and achieves the optimal \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio.
sources.connection: The Periodic Reranking algorithm in OBMRR is explicitly designed to reduce to Perturbed Greedy when \(d=|U|\); understanding the \(1-1/e\) analysis highlights what breaks under reusability and what must be recovered to reach \(1-1/e\).

sources.title: Randomized primal-dual analysis of ranking for online bipartite matching
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a randomized primal-dual (dual-fitting) analysis framework for RANKING, clarifying how to construct dual variables from random ranks to certify \(1-1/e\).
sources.connection: Delong et al. adapt this randomized primal-dual template to periodic reranking under reuse constraints; any attempt to reach \(1-1/e\) for OBMRR likely needs a sharper dual-fitting argument or new dual variables capturing availability windows.

sources.title: Edge-weighted online bipartite matching
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the online correlated selection (OCS) technique to induce negative correlation in online matching decisions, enabling improved competitive ratios in edge-weighted settings.
sources.connection: Delong et al. extend OCS to reusable resources via Online Correlated Rental (OCR); further improvements to OCR/OCS parameters are a plausible route toward \(1-1/e\) for OBMRR.

sources.title: Improved online correlated selection
sources.theorem_or_result: Improves guarantees/parameters for online correlated selection beyond the original OCS construction.
sources.connection: If improved OCS parameters can be translated to the reusable-resource OCR setting, the resulting stronger negative-correlation guarantee \(\gamma\) could improve the 0.505 bound and potentially approach \(1-1/e\).

sources.title: Multiway online correlated selection
sources.theorem_or_result: Extends online correlated selection to multiway choices, providing more flexible correlation structures than pairwise OCS.
sources.connection: OBMRR algorithms may benefit from proposing more than two candidate resources per request; multiway correlated selection could be a building block for such extensions under reuse constraints.

sources.title: Asymptotically optimal competitive ratio for online allocation of reusable resources
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies online allocation with reusable resources in the large-capacity/fractional regime and achieves asymptotically optimal \(1-1/e\)-type guarantees (with appropriate algorithms) under that model.
sources.connection: Shows \(1-1/e\) is achievable for reusable resources when capacities are large or allocations are fractional; highlights the integrality/unit-inventory barrier central to the OBMRR open problem.
